front: Work: this anti-imperialist poem calls its title figure 'Alexander-Nebuchadnezzar' and 'professor of Energy,' ending with 'you lack one thing: God!'
Work: this poem invokes 'the voice of the Bible, or the verse of Walt Whitman' to address the U.S. president
Work: this poem warns 'a thousand cubs of the Spanish lion are roaming free' and accuses its addressee of joining 'the cult of Hercules and the cult of Mammon'
Work: this 1888 collection kickstarted modernismo in Latin American literature; its title ends with an ellipsis
Work: this collection contains 'The Bourgeois King' (a hungry poet forced to crank a music box), 'The Ruby,' and the 'Veil of Queen Mab'
Work: two letters from Juan Valera noting 'mental Gallicism' were included as prologue to this collection
Work: this poem opens 'The sea like a vast silvered mirror reflects the sky like a sheet of zinc' and describes an old sea-dog in 'the siesta of the tropics'
Author: this Nicaraguan poet frequently used the swan as a symbol; described 'the question posed by a white swan's neck'
Work: this collection contains 'To Roosevelt' and a section titled after a bird related to the myth of Leda
back: Songs of Life and Hope (Ruben Dario)

front: Work: in this poem, a fairy maiden guides the speaker through a lush landscape as he repeatedly shouts 'More!' driven by 'the thirst of infinite desire'
Work: in this poem, the speaker compares himself to 'an aged tree' that 'uttered a vague, sweet sound when the breeze caressed me'
Work: this poem describes a princess guarded by 'a hound, a sleepless dragon and a hundred negroes with a hundred lances'; it adapts French alexandrines into 14-syllable meter
Author: this poet claimed 'I think in French!' and said his style was 'Spanish verse passed through the sieve of French verse'
Author: Giannina Braschi's United States of Banana uses this poet's epigraph 'If Segismundo grieves, Hamlet feels it'
Author: Octavio Paz described this poet as more like Victor Hugo than his idol Verlaine in 'The Siren and the Seashell'
Author: in The Autumn of the Patriarch, the dictator asks this poet how he can write so beautifully with the hand that wipes his ass
Work: this poem describes drinking 'droplets of refined French sap' with a 'half-Spanish, half-indigenous mouth'
